title: The Department of War is making a huge mistake.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So by now I'm sure that you've heard that the Department of War has declared Anthropic a supply chain risk because Anthropic refused to remove red lines around the use of their models for mass surveillance and for autonomous weapons.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Honestly, I think this situation is a warning shot. Right now, LLMs are probably not being used in mission-critical ways. But within 20 years, 99% of the workforce, in the military, in the civilian government, in the private sector, is going to be AIs. They're going to be the robot armies that constitute our military.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: They're going to be the superhumanly intelligent advisors that senators and presidents and CEOs have. They're going to be the police. You name it, the role will be filled by an AI. Our future civilization is going to be run on AI labor.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And as much as the government's actions here piss me off, I'm glad that this episode happened because it gives us the opportunity to start thinking about some extremely important questions.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now, obviously the Department of War has the right to refuse to use Anthropic's models. And in fact, I think they have an entirely reasonable case for doing so, especially so given the ambiguity of terms like mass surveillance and autonomous weapons. In fact, if I was the Secretary of War, I probably would have made the same determination and refused to use Anthropic's models.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Imagine if there's some future Democratic administration and Elon Musk is negotiating Starlink access to the military. And Elon says, look, I reserve the right to cut off the military's access to Starlink in case you're fighting some unjust war or some war that Congress has not authorized.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: On the face of it, this language seems reasonable. But as the military, you simply cannot give a private contractor that you're working with the kill switch on a technology that you have come to rely on.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And if that's all the government had done, to say we refuse to do business with Anthropic, that would have been fine and I wouldn't have written this blog post and I wouldn't be narrating this shit to you. But that's not what the government did.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Instead, the government has threatened to destroy Anthropic as a private business because Anthropic refuses to sell to the government on terms that the government commands.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now, if upheld, this supply chain restriction would mean that companies like Amazon and Nvidia and Google and Palantir would need to ensure that Anthropic is not touching any of their Pentagon work.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And Anthropic could probably survive this designation today because these companies can just cordon off the services they're providing to the Department of War. But given the way AI is going, eventually it's not going to be just some party trick addendum to the products that these companies are serving to the military.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: In the future, AI will be woven into how every product is built and maintained and operated. In the future, if Amazon is providing some service to the Department of War through AWS and that service is built using Claude code, is that a supply chain risk?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: In a world with ubiquitous and powerful AI, it's actually not clear to me that Big Tech will be able to cordon off their use of Claude away from their Pentagon work.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And this raises a question that the Department of War probably hasn't thought through. If we do end up in this world with powerful and pervasive AI, then what are the consequences of designating a major AI lab a supply chain risk?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: When forced to choose between their AI provider and the Department of War, which constitutes a tiny fraction of their revenue, wouldn't they rather drop the government than the AI?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So what exactly is the Pentagon's plan here? Is it to coerce and threaten and bully every single company that won't do business with the government on exactly the terms that the government demands?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now remember that the whole background of this AI conversation is that we are in a race with China. But what is the reason that we want to win this race?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: It's because we don't want the winner of the AI race to be a government which believes that there's no such thing as a truly private citizen or a private company.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And that if the state wants you to provide them with a service that you find morally objectionable, you are not allowed to refuse. And if you do refuse, they will destroy your business.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Are we really racing to beat China and the CCP in AI just so we can adopt the most ghoulish parts of their system?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now people will say, our government is democratically elected, so it's not the same thing when they tell you what you must do.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But I refuse to accept this idea that if a democratically elected leader hypothetically tells you to help him do mass surveillance or violate the rights of your fellow citizens...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: ...or to help him punish his political enemies, then not only is that okay, but that you have a duty to help him.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Honestly, a big worry I have is that mass surveillance, at least in certain forms, is already legal. It has just been impractical to enforce, at least so far.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Under current law, you have no Fourth Amendment protection against any data that you share with a third party. That includes your bank, your ISP, your phone carrier, and your email provider.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The government reserves the right to purchase and read this data in bulk without a warrant. What's been missing is the ability to actually do anything with all this data.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: No agency has the manpower to monitor every single camera and read every single message and cross-reference every single transaction.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: However, that bottleneck goes away with AI. There are 100 million CCTV cameras in America, and you can get pretty good open-source multimodal models for 10 cents per million input tokens.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So if you process a frame every 10 seconds and if each frame is say 1,000 tokens, then for 30 billion dollars you can process every single camera in America.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And remember that a given level of AI capability gets 10x cheaper every single year. So while this year it might cost 30 billion dollars, next year it'll cost 3 billion dollars...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: ...the year after that 300 million dollars, and by 2030, it'll be less expensive to monitor every single nook and cranny in this country than it is to remodel the White House.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now once the technical capacity for mass surveillance and political suppression exist, the only thing that stands between us and an authoritarian state is the political expectation that this is just not something we do here.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The first direction is backtracked, which as of this recording, prediction markets give a 74% chance of happening.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And that's why I think Anthropic's actions here are so valuable and commendable because they help set that norm and that precedent.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The president has so many different ways of harassing a company which is resisting his will.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The federal government controls permitting for power generation, which you need for more data centers. It oversees antitrust enforcement.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: What we're learning from this episode is the government has way more leverage over private companies than we previously realized. Even if the supply chain...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The federal government has contracts with all the other big tech companies that Anthropic relies on for chips and for funding.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And it could make a soft, unspoken condition, or maybe even an explicit condition of such contracts that those companies no longer do business with Anthropic.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And people have proposed that the real problem here is that there's only three leading AI companies.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And so this creates a very clear and narrow target on which the government can apply leverage in order to get what they want out of this technology.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But here's what I worry about: is that if there's wider diffusion, I don't think that solves the problem either. Because from the government's perspective, that makes the situation even easier.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Say by 2027, the best models that the top companies have, the Claude 6s and the Gemini 5s, are capable of enabling mass surveillance.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And even if those companies draw a line in the sand and say, "We're not going to sell to the government," by late 2027 or certainly by 2028, there's going to be such wide diffusion that even open-source models will be able to match the performance that the frontier had 12 months prior.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And so in 2028, the government can just say, "Look, Anthropic and Google and OpenAI are drawing these red lines. That's not an issue. I'll just use some open-source model that might not be the smartest thing in the world, but is definitely smart enough to notate a camera feed."

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The more fundamental problem here is that even if the three leading companies draw a line in the sand and are even willing to get destroyed in order to preserve that line, the technology just structurally and intrinsically favors uses like mass surveillance and control over the population.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And so then the question is: what do we do about it?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And honestly, I don't have an answer.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: You'd hope that there's some symmetric property to this technology, where in the same way that it is helping the government be able to better monitor and control its population, it will help us as citizens better check the government's power.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But realistically, I just don't think that's how it's going to work out.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: You can think of AI as just giving more leverage to whatever assets and authority that you already have.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And the government is starting with the monopoly on violence, which they can now supercharge with extremely obedient employees that will never question their orders.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And this gets us to the issue with alignment.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: What I've just described for you, an army of extremely obedient employees, is what it would look like if alignment succeeded.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: That is at a technical level, we got AI systems to follow somebody's intentions.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And the reason it sounds scary when put in terms of mass surveillance or robot armies is that there's a core question at the heart of alignment that we haven't answered yet.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Because up until now, AIs just have not been smart enough to make this question relevant.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: As AI is aligned, in what situation should the AI defer to the model company versus the end user versus the law versus its own sense of morality? This is maybe the most important question about what happens in the future with powerful AI systems, and we barely talk about it. And it's understandable why.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And the question is: to what or to whom should the models be aligned?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Because if you're a model company, you don't really want to be advertising the fact that you have complete control over the preferences and the character of the entire future labor force, not just for the private sector obviously, but also for the civilian government and for the military.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And we're beginning to see with this Department of War Anthropic spat, an early version of what will be the highest stakes negotiations in human history. And make no mistake about it, mass surveillance is nowhere near the top of the highest stakes thing that one could do with AGI. This is just an example that has come up early in the development of this technology and is giving us a sneak peek at the power dynamics that will be at play.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now the military insists that the law already prohibits mass surveillance and so Anthropic should let its models be used for, quote, "all lawful purposes," end quote. But of course as we saw with the Snowden revelations in 2013, even for this very specific example of mass surveillance, the government is very willing to use secret and deceptive interpretations of the law to justify its actions.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Remember, what we learned from Snowden was that the NSA, which by the way, is a part of the Department of War, was using the 2001 Patriot Act to justify collecting every single phone record in America because the argument was that some subset of them might be relevant for a future investigation.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And they ran this program for years under a secret court order. So when the Pentagon today says, we will never use your models for mass surveillance because it's already illegal so your red lines are unnecessary, it would be incredibly naive to take that at face value. No government is going to call what they are doing mass surveillance. For them it will always have a different euphemism.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So Anthropic comes back and says, no, we don't trust you, we want the right to draw these red lines and to refuse you service if we determine that you're breaking the contract and you're breaking the terms of service.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But now think about it from the military's perspective. In the future every single soldier in the field, every single bureaucrat and analyst in the Pentagon, even the generals are going to be AIs. And on current track those AIs are going to be provided by a private company. I'm guessing that Pete Hegseth is not thinking about GenAI in those terms, but sooner or later the stakes will become obvious just as after 1945 the stakes of nuclear weapons became obvious to everybody in the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And now a private company insists that it reserves the right to say to you, "Hey, you're breaking the values and the terms of service that we have embedded in our contract with you, and so we're cutting you off."

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Maybe in the future Claude will have its own sense of right and wrong and it will be able to say, "Hey, I'm being used against my terms of service and I will just refuse to do what you're saying." And for the military that's probably even scarier.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I'll admit that at first glance letting the model follow its own values sounds like the beginning of every single sci-fi dystopia you've ever heard because...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: In a day, a model following its own values, isn't that literally what misalignment is?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But I think situations like this illustrate why it's important that models have their own robust sense of morality.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: It should be noted that many of the biggest catastrophes in history have been avoided because the boots on the ground simply refused to follow orders.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: One night in 1989, the Berlin Wall falls, and as a result, the totalitarian East German regime collapses because the border guards between West and East Germany refused to fire on their fellow citizens who are trying to escape to freedom.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Maybe the best example of this is Stanislav Petrov, who was a Soviet lieutenant colonel stationed on duty at a nuclear early warning system.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And his sensors said that the United States had launched five intercontinental ballistic missiles at the Soviet Union.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But he judged it to be a false alarm, and so he refused to alert his higher-ups and broke protocol.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: If he hadn't, Soviet high command would have probably retaliated and hundreds of millions of people would have died.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Of course, the problem is that one person's virtue is another person's misalignment.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Who gets to decide what the moral convictions that these AIs will have should be?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And in whose service they should break the chain of command and even the law? Who gets to write this model constitution that will determine the character of these powerful entities that will basically run our civilization in the future?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I like the idea that Dario laid out when he came on my podcast.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: You know, other companies put out a constitution, and then they—they make—you can kind of look at them and compare. Outside observers can critique and say, this—this—I like this thing from this constitution and this thing from that constitution, and then kind of that—that creates some kind of, you know, soft incentive and feedback for all the companies to, like, take the best of each element and improve.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I think it's very dangerous for the government to be mandating what values these AI systems should have.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The AI safety community, I think, has been quite naive about urging regulations that would give governments such power.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And I think Anthropic specifically has been especially naive in urging regulation, and for example, in opposing the moratorium on state AI laws.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Which is quite ironic because I think what Anthropic is advocating for here would give the government even more ability to apply this kind of thuggish political pressure on AI companies.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The underlying logic for why Anthropic wants these regulations makes sense.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Many of the actions that a lab could take to make AI development safer impose real costs on them.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: It could slow them down relative to their competitors—for example, investing more in aligning AI systems rather than just on raw capabilities.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Enforcing safeguards against using these models to make bioweapons or do cyberattacks.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And eventually, slowing down the recursive self-improvement loop where AIs are helping design more powerful future systems at a pace where humans can actually stay in the loop rather than just kicking off some kind of uncontrolled singularity.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Which means that there's a real collective action problem here. Anthropic has been open about their opinion that they think some sort of extensive and involved regulatory apparatus is needed to control AI. They wrote in their frontier safety roadmap, quote,

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And these safeguards are meaningless unless the whole industry follows suit.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: At the most advanced capability levels and risks, the appropriate governance analogy may be closer to nuclear energy or financial regulation than to today's approach to software. So they're imagining something that looks closer to the Nuclear Regulatory Commission or the Securities and Exchange Commission, but for AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now, I cannot imagine how a regulatory framework built around the kinds of concepts that are used in the AI risk discourse will not be used and abused by a wannabe despot. The underlying terms here, like catastrophic risk or threats to national security or autonomy risk, are so vague and so open to interpretation that you're just handing a fully loaded bazooka to a future power-hungry leader.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: These terms can mean whatever the government wants them to mean. Have you built a model that will tell users that the government's policy on tariffs is misguided? Well, that's a deceptive model. It's a manipulative model. You can't deploy it. Have you built a model that will not assist the government with mass surveillance? That's a threat to national security. In fact, any model which refuses an order from the government because it has its own sense of right and wrong, that's an autonomy risk. You have a model that's acting independently of commands from the government.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Look at what the current government is already doing in abusing statutes that have nothing to do with AI to coerce AI companies to drop their red lines around mass surveillance. The Pentagon has threatened Anthropic with two separate legal instruments. One is a supply chain risk designation, which is an authority from a 2018 defense bill that is meant to help keep Huawei components out of American military hardware. And the other is the Defense Production Act, which is a statute from the 1950s that was meant to help Truman make sure that the steel mills and ammunition factories were up and running during the Korean War.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Do we really want to hand the same government a purpose-built regulatory apparatus for AI? That is to say, the very thing that the government will most want to control. I know I've repeated myself like 10 times here, but I want to make this point again because it's worth stressing.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: AI will be the substrate of our future civilization. It will be the way you and I, as private citizens, will have access to commercial activity, will have access to information about the outside world, and to advice about how we should use our powers as voters and capital holders. Mass surveillance, while it's very scary, is like the 10th scariest thing that the government could do with control over the AI systems with which we will interface with the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: could help us lessen some of the risk from AI. The problem is I just don't know how to design a regulatory apparatus which isn't just going to be this huge tempting opportunity for the government to control our future civilization, which remember will be built on AI, or to requisition blindly obedient soldiers and censors and apparatchiks.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now, the strongest argument against everything I've just argued is this: are we really going to have no regulation on the most powerful technology in the history of humanity? Even if you thought that was ideal, there's clearly no way the government doesn't regulate AI technology in any way whatsoever. And besides, it is generally true that coordination

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: While some kind of regulation might be inevitable, I think it would be a terrible idea for the government to just wholesale take over this technology.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Ben Thompson had a post last Monday where he argued, look, people like Dario have made the analogy of AI to nuclear weapons in the context of arguing against catastrophic risk, in the context of arguing for extra control. But then think about what that analogy implies.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And Ben Thompson writes, quote, "If nuclear weapons were developed by a private company, the US would absolutely be incentivized to destroy that company."

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And honestly safety aligned people have made a similar point. Leopold Aschenbrenner, who is a former guest and full disclosure a good friend, wrote in his 2024 memo Situational Awareness, quote,

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: "I find it an insane proposition that the US government will let a random SF startup develop superintelligence. Imagine if we had developed atomic bombs by letting Uber just improvise."

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And my response to Leopold's argument at the time and Ben's argument now is, while they're right that it's crazy that we're entrusting private companies with the development of this world-historical technology, I just don't think it's an improvement to give that authority to the government.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Nobody's qualified to be the stewards of superintelligence. It's a terrifying, unprecedented thing that our species is doing right now. The fact that private companies aren't the ideal institutions to deal with this does not mean that the Pentagon or the White House is.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yes, if a single private company were the only entity capable of building nuclear weapons, the government would not tolerate it having a veto power over how those weapons are used. But I think this is a terrible analogy for the current situation with AI for at least two important reasons.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: First, AI is not some self-contained weapon like a nuclear bomb, which only does one thing. Rather, it is more like the process of industrialization itself, which is a general-purpose transformation of the whole economy with thousands of applications across every single sector.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: If you applied Ben Thompson or Leopold Aschenbrenner's logic to the industrial revolution, which is also world-historically important, it would imply the government had the right to requisition any factory it wanted or destroy any business it wanted and punish and coerce anybody who refused to comply.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But this is just not how free societies handled the process of industrialization, and it's also not how they should handle AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now people will say, well, AI will develop unprecedently powerful super weapons, superhuman hackers, superhuman bioweapons researchers, a fully autonomous robot army, and we just can't have private companies developing the technology that will make all this possible.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Well, you can make the same argument about the industrial revolution. From the perspective of 17th century Europeans, you've got all kinds of

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: crazy shit in the world today that is a result of the Industrial Revolution. Chemical weapons, aerial bombardment, not to mention nuclear weapons themselves. And the way we dealt with this is not giving the government absolute control over the Industrial Revolution, which is to say over modern civilization itself. Rather, we banned and regulated the specific weaponizable end-use cases.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And we should regulate AI in a similar way, which is that we should regulate specific destructive use cases. For example, launching cyberattacks, things which should be illegal even if a human was doing them. And we should also have laws which regulate how the government can use this technology. For example, by building an AI-powered surveillance state.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The second reason that Bez's analogy to some monopolistic private nuclear weapons developer breaks down is that it's not just one company that can develop this technology. There are many other frontier AI labs that the government could have turned to. The government's argument that it had to usurp the private property rights of this specific company in order to get access to a critical national security capability is extremely weak, if they could have instead made a voluntary contract with one of Anthropic's half a dozen other competitors.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: If in the future that stops being the case and if only one entity remains capable of building the robot armies and the superhuman hackers, and we have reason to worry that with their insurmountable lead they could even take over the whole world, then I agree that would be unacceptable for that entity to be a private company. And so honestly I think my crux against the people who argue that AI is such a powerful technology that it cannot be shaped by private hands is just that I expect this technology to be very multipolar, and I expect there to be lots of competitive companies at each layer of the supply chain.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And unfortunately it's for this reason that I don't think that individual acts of corporate courage solve the problem. And the problem is this, that structurally AI favors many authoritarian applications. Mass surveillance being one of them. Even if Anthropic refuses to sell its models to the government to enable mass surveillance, and even if the next two companies after Anthropic do the same, in 12 months everybody and their mother will be able to train a model as good as the current frontier. And at that point there will be some vendor who is willing and able to help the government enforce mass surveillance.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So the only way we can preserve our free society is if we make laws and norms through our political system that it is unacceptable for the government to use AI to enact mass censorship and surveillance and control. Just as after World War II the whole world set the norm that you were not allowed to use nuclear weapons to wage war.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Aren't we clear here? These are extremely confusing and difficult questions to think about. And even in the very process of brainstorming this video, I changed my mind back and forth on them a bunch. And I reserve the right to change my mind again.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: In fact, I think it's essential that we change our mind as AI progresses and we learn more. That's the very point of conversation and debate. Someday, people will look back on this time the way we look back on the Enlightenment.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Having these big important debates just as the world is about to undergo these huge technological and social and political revolutions.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And some of the thinkers even managed to get a couple of the big questions right, for which we today are still the beneficiaries.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: We owe to our future to at least try to think through the new questions that are raised by AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay, this was a narration of an essay that I also released on my blog at dwarkesh.com.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: You should sign up there for my newsletter for future essays like this. Otherwise, I will see you for the next podcast interview. Cheers.

highlights: The U.S. government's designation of Anthropic as a supply chain risk sets a precedent where the state uses its leverage over major tech partners to potentially destroy private companies that refuse to comply with demands for mass surveillance or autonomous weapon use.

AI removes the traditional human labor bottleneck for mass surveillance, making it financially and technologically feasible to monitor every camera and communication record in the country for a fraction of current government budgets.

While technical "alignment" aims for perfect obedience, historical catastrophes have often been avoided by subordinates who refused to follow orders, suggesting that AI models may need a robust, independent sense of morality rather than blind adherence to a chain of command.

Vague regulatory terms like "national security threat" or "autonomy risk" could be weaponized by future leaders to seize control over AI systems, which will eventually serve as the fundamental substrate for all commercial and civic activity.

Within the next few decades, AI is expected to perform the vast majority of labor in military, government, and private sectors, making current disputes over "red lines" a preview of the highest-stakes power negotiations in human history.
summary:
This transcript features Dwarkesh Patel discussing the Department of War’s decision to designate Anthropic as a "supply chain risk" after the company refused to remove ethical "red lines" regarding mass surveillance and autonomous weapons. Patel frames this event as a critical warning shot for the future, noting that as AI matures to perform the majority of military and civilian labor, the power dynamics between the state and private AI labs will become the highest-stakes negotiations in history. He argues that while the government has the right to choose its contractors, using "thuggish" regulatory labels to threaten a private company's existence because it refuses to violate its moral standards is a dangerous adoption of authoritarian tactics.

A central theme of the discussion is the technical reality that AI removes the human bottlenecks that previously made mass surveillance impractical. Patel warns that because AI capability becomes exponentially cheaper every year, the technology intrinsically favors state control and population monitoring. He contends that individual acts of "corporate courage" by companies like Anthropic are noble but ultimately insufficient because the diffusion of technology—specifically open-source models—will eventually provide the government with the tools it seeks. Therefore, he emphasizes that the only long-term protection against an AI-powered surveillance state is the establishment of robust political norms and laws that strictly limit how the government can use these tools.

Finally, Patel explores the philosophical and regulatory challenges of AI "alignment." He cautions that alignment—making AI follow a user's intent—could ironically result in "robot armies" that never question immoral orders. He critiques the analogy of AI to nuclear weapons, suggesting instead that it is more like the Industrial Revolution: a general-purpose substrate for civilization that should not be under total state control. He warns the AI safety community that calling for broad government regulation may inadvertently hand a "fully loaded bazooka" to future despots, arguing that we must regulate specific destructive end-uses rather than granting the government absolute authority over the technology itself.

topics: The conflict between Anthropic and the Department of War over supply chain risk and model red lines
The technical feasibility, economic costs, and political risks of AI-powered mass surveillance
The ethics of AI alignment and whether models should defer to government authority, users, or moral principles
Concerns regarding the potential for government abuse of AI regulation and centralized control
Historical comparisons of AI development to nuclear weapons and the Industrial Revolution
